text: The case is a eighty-five-year-old male who presented with rectal bleeding and constipation. Colonoscopy showed the mass in rectosigmoid colon, which was proven to be an adenocarcinoma by biopsies. CT scan revealed the proliferative mass in rectosigmoid colon without any distant metastasis. Therefore, laparoscopic lower anterior resection with complete mesorectal excision was planned.

Four trocars are placed in the abdominal wall. The mass is visible with its attachment to the dome of the bladder. The mesentery is dissected using a hook cautery. A mesenteric window is opened, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. The artery is ligated with two hemoclips and cut, followed by the vein, which is also ligated with hemoclips and cut using an energy device. Dissection continues laterally with an incision along the white line of Toldt, mobilizing the colon. The dome of the bladder, invaded by cancer, is included in the specimen to ensure safe surgical margins.

The surgeon transects the rectum using surgical staplers. The superior wall of the bladder is repaired with a single layer continuous suture. A colorectal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler, followed by a negative air leak test indicating no leaks.
The patient was discharged uneventfully on sixth post-operative day.
